title: Space Tech Briefing — June 28, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space cadets and cosmic enthusiasts! This is your daily dose of the universe, coming to you live on June 28, 2026. I’m thrilled to bring you the latest and greatest from the final frontier!

First up, as we eagerly anticipate what new discoveries today might bring, let's take a moment to look back at a truly monumental recent development. The European Space Agency's Euclid telescope has delivered an absolutely breathtaking gift to humanity! Just a few days ago, on June 24th, ESA unveiled the largest high-resolution visible light image ever taken of the Milky Way's core. This isn't just any picture; it's a colossal 6-gigapixel mosaic, revealing over 60 million stars in a single scene. Imagine that – sixty million stars!

This incredible image, captured back in March 2025 during a marathon 26-hour observation, offers unprecedented detail of our galaxy's incredibly dense central region. Euclid pointed its camera straight at the galactic bulge, stitching together nine distinct shots, each one covering a patch of sky larger than a full moon. To ensure we see the full vibrant spectrum, teams even supplemented Euclid's visible light data with observations from the ground-based Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope. It's a true masterpiece, giving us a fresh, incredibly detailed look at our cosmic home!

And from grand cosmic vistas to inspiring human stories, let's turn our attention to some future space explorers whose words have already made it to the stars. In a wonderful initiative by the French space agency CNES, nearly 3,000 young people, aged between 5 and 15, participated in a poetry contest centered on dreams and space. The "Astropoems" competition truly captured imaginations! Four lucky winners saw their beautiful verses take an actual journey to the International Space Station. Imagine the pride – your own words, orbiting above Earth, among the stars! It's a fantastic way to inspire the next generation of scientists, engineers, and dreamers.

What a start to our day, with both incredible scientific achievement and heartwarming human inspiration! That's all for your morning briefing, but the universe is always buzzing with activity. Stay curious, keep looking up, and we'll catch you next time for more space news!
